debates.question: Why is each prince's identical offering recorded separately at full length?

sides.position: To show God's particular notice of each tribe and each offerer - the equal honor and willing devotion of all the tribes, each recorded by name and accepted, a figure of the church where each member's gift is equally valued (Psalm 149:4; 2 Corinthians 9:7; 1 Corinthians 14:40)

schools.summary: The peace offerings of the prince of Naphtali: 'and for a sacrifice of peace offerings, two oxen, five rams, five he goats, five lambs of the first year: this was the offering of Ahira the son of Enan' — the prince of Naphtali closing his offering with the peace offerings (the for a sacrifice of peace offerings, the offering of fellowship, Leviticus 3, the two oxen, five rams, five he goats, five lambs, the abundant peace offerings, the this was the offering of Ahira the son of Enan, the prince named, his gift recorded, the figure, the peace offerings of communion and fellowship with God, the feast that crowns the offering, the peace offering a figure of the communion with God through Christ our peace, 'for he is our peace,' Ephesians 2:14, 'having made peace through the blood of his cross,' Colossians 1:20, the fellowship that flows from the atonement, the prince's whole offering recorded by name, his devotion noted by God, none slighted); the abundant peace offerings (the fellowship offering, Leviticus 3) — the peace offerings (the communion); 'this was the offering of Ahira the son of Enan' (the prince named and his gift recorded, the figure, communion through Christ, Ephesians 2:14, the providence, Naphtali's offering complete) — Gill.

schools.summary: The dedication of the altar by the princes: 'this was the dedication of the altar, in the day when it was anointed, by the princes of Israel: twelve chargers of silver, twelve silver bowls, twelve spoons of gold' — the dedication — 'this was the dedication of the altar... by the princes of Israel': the whole offering of the twelve princes was the dedication of the altar, the gifts presented when it was anointed and first put to use (the witnesses noting the significance — the dedication of the altar by the willing offering of the princes, the twelve chargers, bowls, and spoons summed, the whole given at the altar's consecration, the lesson — the willing consecration of God's house and its service, the princes leading the people in the free offering for God's worship, the altar dedicated by the gifts of the willing-hearted, the gladness of giving to God's house, 'the people rejoiced, for that they offered willingly... with perfect heart they offered willingly to the LORD,' 1 Chronicles 29:9, the figure — the consecration of the altar, the place of sacrifice, a figure of Christ the altar and the sacrifice, Hebrews 13:10, by the willing offerings of God's people, the dedication that sets apart the holy things for God's use, the willing gifts that honor him, the example of the leaders giving first and most, the princes setting the pattern of devotion, the figure of the consecration of all to God, the altar and its service dedicated by the free and equal offerings of the tribes); the altar dedicated when anointed (the gifts at its consecration, set apart for sacred use) — the dedication (the altar consecrated); 'by the princes of Israel: twelve chargers of silver, twelve silver bowls, twelve spoons of gold' (the summed gifts of the twelve princes, the whole willing offering, 1 Chronicles 29:9, the figure, the willing consecration of God's house, Hebrews 13:10, the providence, the dedication summed, the totals to follow, vv.85-88) — Clarke, Gill.

debates.question: What does the dedication of the altar by the princes' offering teach?

schools.summary: The total weight of the silver vessels: 'each charger of silver weighing an hundred and thirty shekels, each bowl seventy: all the silver vessels weighed two thousand and four hundred shekels, after the shekel of the sanctuary' — the silver vessels summed: each charger 130 shekels, each bowl 70, the whole 2,400 shekels by the sanctuary standard (the each charger... an hundred and thirty shekels, each bowl seventy, the weight of each vessel, the all the silver vessels weighed two thousand and four hundred shekels, the total weight, the after the shekel of the sanctuary, the divine standard, the figure, the exact reckoning of the silver, the metal of redemption, weighed to the shekel, the careful accounting of all that was offered, every vessel weighed and recorded, the silver of redemption, Exodus 30:16, the truth that God reckons exactly what is given, none unaccounted, the whole offering precisely summed); the weight of each vessel (130 and 70 shekels) — the weight (each vessel); 'all the silver vessels weighed two thousand and four hundred shekels' (the total weight by the sanctuary standard, the silver of redemption, Exodus 30:16, the figure, the exact reckoning, the providence, the silver summed, the gold to follow, vv.86) — Gill.

schools.summary: The total of the golden spoons of incense: 'the golden spoons were twelve, full of incense, weighing ten shekels apiece, after the shekel of the sanctuary: all the gold of the spoons was an hundred and twenty shekels' — the twelve golden spoons, full of incense, each ten shekels, the whole 120 shekels of gold (the the golden spoons were twelve, full of incense, the twelve spoons of prayer, Revelation 8:3, the weighing ten shekels apiece, the weight of each, the all the gold of the spoons was an hundred and twenty shekels, the total gold, the figure, the twelve golden spoons full of incense, the prayers of the twelve tribes, the incense of intercession from all the people, Revelation 5:8, the gold the precious offering, the incense the prayer, the figure of the prayers of all the saints offered with the much incense of Christ's intercession, Revelation 8:3-4, the whole offering of prayer summed, the incense of the tribes weighed and recorded); the twelve golden spoons of incense (the prayer of the tribes, Revelation 5:8) — the spoons (the incense); 'all the gold of the spoons was an hundred and twenty shekels' (the total gold, the offering of prayer summed, the figure, the prayers of all the saints, Revelation 8:3-4, the providence, the gold summed, the burnt offerings to follow, vv.87) — Gill.

schools.summary: The total of the peace offerings, closing the dedication: 'and all the oxen for the sacrifice of the peace offerings were twenty and four bullocks, the rams sixty, the he goats sixty, the lambs of the first year sixty. This was the dedication of the altar, after that it was anointed' — the peace offerings summed: 24 bullocks, 60 rams, 60 he goats, 60 lambs, each prince bringing two oxen and five of each of the rest (the twenty and four bullocks, the rams sixty, the he goats sixty, the lambs sixty, the abundant peace offerings of all the tribes, the this was the dedication of the altar, after that it was anointed, the close of the dedication, the figure, the abundant peace offerings of fellowship, the communion of all the tribes with God, the great feast of fellowship that crowned the dedication, the peace offerings most numerous, the fellowship with God through the atonement abounding, Ephesians 2:14, the dedication closed with the offerings of communion, the joyful fellowship that flows from the consecrated altar, the whole dedication summed and complete); the abundant peace offerings summed (the fellowship of all the tribes, Leviticus 3) — the peace offerings (the communion); 'this was the dedication of the altar, after that it was anointed' (the dedication complete, the close of the offering, Ephesians 2:14, the figure, the fellowship crowning the dedication, the providence, the dedication summed, the voice from the mercy seat to follow, vv.89) — Clarke, Gill.

schools.summary: The voice of God from the mercy seat between the cherubim: 'and when Moses was gone into the tabernacle of the congregation to speak with him, then he heard the voice of one speaking unto him from off the mercy seat that was upon the ark of testimony, from between the two cherubims: and he spake unto him' — the voice from the mercy seat — 'he heard the voice of one speaking unto him from off the mercy seat... from between the two cherubims': when Moses went into the tabernacle to speak with God, he heard God's voice from the mercy seat, between the cherubim (the witnesses noting the significance — the place of God's speaking and communion, the mercy seat, the golden lid of the ark covering the law, the place where God promised to meet and commune with Moses, Exodus 25:22, the voice from between the cherubim, the throne of God's presence, the lesson — that God communes with his people from the mercy seat, the place of atonement, the voice came not from the law within the ark, but from the mercy seat above it, that covered the law, God speaks to his people from the place of mercy and atonement, not from the bare law that condemns, the figure — the mercy seat, Hebrew kapporeth, the place of covering and propitiation, a figure of Christ the propitiation, 'whom God hath set forth to be a propitiation (hilasterion, the mercy seat) through faith in his blood,' Romans 3:25, 'the mercyseat... of which we cannot now speak particularly,' Hebrews 9:5, 'he is the propitiation for our sins,' 1 John 2:2, the truth that God meets and communes with us in Christ the mercy seat, who covers the broken law and reconciles us to God, the cherubim looking down upon the mercy seat, the angels desiring to look into the things of redemption, 1 Peter 1:12, the figure of all communion with God grounded in the atonement, God speaking to us from the throne of grace, 'let us therefore come boldly unto the throne of grace,' Hebrews 4:16, the climax of the dedication, that God who is now worshipped at the altar speaks from the mercy seat, the whole tabernacle service crowned with the communion of God with his people through the propitiation); Moses entering to speak with God (the communion sought, Exodus 25:22) — the communion (Moses with God); the voice from the mercy seat between the cherubim (God speaking from the place of atonement, Romans 3:25; Hebrews 9:5) — the figure (Christ the mercy seat, the throne of grace, Hebrews 4:16, the providence, God communing from the mercy seat, the dedication crowned) — Calvin, Clarke, Wesley, Gill.

debates.question: What does the voice from the mercy seat signify?

sides.position: God speaking and communing with his people from the place of atonement (Exodus 25:22), not from the bare law but from the mercy seat that covers it - a figure of Christ the propitiation (the hilasterion) through whom God meets and communes with us (Romans 3:25; Hebrews 9:5; 1 John 2:2), the throne of grace to which we come boldly (Hebrews 4:16)

schools.summary: The offering of the princes when the tabernacle was finished — 'on the day that Moses had fully set up the tabernacle, and had anointed it, and sanctified it' (the offering coming not till all was finished, the dwelling and the camp ordered about it, the gifts crowning a completed work, Henry); 'the day' standing for the time or season, the princes offering on successive days not one (Wesley); the tabernacle, altar, and vessels anointed with the holy oil and set apart (Ex 40:9, Lev 8:10), consecrated before their service began; a second free contribution after the great giving for the building (Ex 35:21), the people's devotion going on to furnish the service (Calvin); the princes leading in giving as in rule, offering at their own charge.

schools.summary: The princes as the offerers — 'the princes of Israel, heads of the house of their fathers... the princes of the tribes... offered' (the twelve tribal princes, the same men set over the numbering of ch1-2 and made captains over the hosts, the chief men of each tribe, Keil and Delitzsch/Gill); the leaders who held the foremost place in rule taking also the foremost in giving, leading their tribes in liberality as in the camp and march; the representative character — though the princes named as offerers, the gifts in truth from the whole tribe, the prince presenting what his tribe subscribed; offering in the order and on the days afterward set down (Wesley); leaders setting the example in devotion, the people stirred to follow when those in high place give gladly to God.

the prince offers for his tribe

schools.summary: The wagons and oxen for the tabernacle's service — 'six covered wagons, and twelve oxen; a wagon for two of the princes, and for each one an ox' (the twelve princes together providing six covered or tilted wagons and twelve draught oxen, each pair joining for a wagon, each prince giving an ox); the word 'covered' variously rendered, the ancient versions guessing, the wagons at least roofed, fit to protect what they bore (Clarke); the purpose — dedicated for the conveyance of the tabernacle, whose pillars, boards, and heavy parts could not be borne on men's shoulders but needed wheels for safe transport (Calvin, Wesley); the princes providing the practical means by which the holy dwelling could be moved, devotion furnishing what the worship of God requires; brought 'before the LORD,' before the tabernacle, a freewill gift.

devotion furnishes the service

schools.summary: God's word approving the gift — 'the LORD spake unto Moses' (out of the tabernacle, before which the wagons and oxen had been brought, God answering from his dwelling, the place of his manifested presence where he promised to commune with Moses, Ex 25:22, Gill); the freewill offering not left to Moses' own judgment but God himself giving direction, showing he regarded and accepted the gift and would order its use to his service; the word both approving the offering and instructing how it should be applied; the condescension of God who takes notice of his people's gifts and speaks concerning them, nothing offered in faith overlooked; Moses doing nothing of his own but waiting on God's word; the gift honoured by being made the subject of a divine command.

schools.summary: The gift accepted and assigned to the Levites — 'take it of them, that they may be to do the service of the tabernacle... give them unto the Levites, to every man according to his service'; 'take it of them' showing the freewill offering was according to God's mind, well pleasing, prompted by his own Spirit, God not refusing what is offered in faith (Gill); the wagons and oxen put to the carriage of the tabernacle and given to the Levites who bore that charge; 'according to his service' — shared as their burdens required, most to those with the heaviest things to carry, fewer to those with less, apportioned to the need (Clarke, Wesley); God's service furnished and each company of Levites supplied according to its work, neither overburdened nor wanting; the princes' liberality lightening the Levites' labour.

themes: take it of them
the service of the tabernacle

according to his service

schools.summary: Moses' obedient distribution — 'Moses took the wagons and the oxen, and gave them unto the Levites' (exactly as directed in v5, receiving the princes' offering and distributing it to the Levites for the service of the tabernacle, the steward doing just as his Lord commanded, neither adding nor withholding, Gill); the faithfulness of Moses in this small matter as in great ones, waiting for God's word concerning the gift and then performing it without delay, as careful to obey in the ordering of wagons as in the weightier things of the law; the gift of the princes passing, by God's appointment and Moses' hand, to the Levites who needed it, the liberality of the leaders becoming the help of the ministers; God's house served when each does his part.

themes: Moses took the wagons and the oxen
gave them unto the Levites
prompt obedience
the gift passed to the ministers

schools.summary: The portion of the Gershonites — 'two wagons and four oxen he gave unto the sons of Gershon, according unto their service' (the family of Gershon, the eldest son of Levi, given two of the six wagons and four of the twelve oxen, Gill); the reason — the Gershonites had the carrying of the curtains, coverings, and hangings (Num 4:25), bulky and cumbersome yet not of great weight, so that two wagons sufficed (Clarke); the distribution 'according unto their service' apportioning the wagons to the nature of each family's burden, the Gershonites' load large in bulk but light in weight; God's ordering matching the help to the task, neither more nor less than needed; the soft furnishings of the holy place provided for in their carriage.

themes: two wagons and four oxen
unto the sons of Gershon
according unto their service
the help matched to the burden

schools.summary: The larger portion of the Merarites — 'four wagons and eight oxen he gave unto the sons of Merari, according unto their service, under the hand of Ithamar' (all the remaining wagons and oxen, double the Gershonites' number, Gill); the reason — the Merarites carrying the boards, bars, pillars, sockets, and pins (Num 4:31-32), the heavy timber and metal framework, so weighty a load requiring twice the wagons (Clarke); 'according unto their service' apportioning the help to the weight of the task, the heaviest-laden family given the most; the oversight 'under the hand of Ithamar the son of Aaron the priest,' the service of Gershon and Merari overseen by the priesthood; God furnishing his service so the heaviest burdens were the best supplied, none left to bear beyond his help.

schools.summary: The Kohathites bearing the holy things on their shoulders — 'unto the sons of Kohath he gave none: because the service of the sanctuary belonging unto them was that they should bear upon their shoulders' (no wagons to Kohath, all disposed to Gershon and Merari, not from neglect but for the reason given, Gill); the Kohathites' charge — the most holy things, the ark, table, candlestick, and altars (Num 4:15), to be carried on the shoulders, borne by hand not loaded on wagons (Clarke); because of the greater worth and holiness of the things they carried, the sacred vessels not drawn by beasts like common freight but borne reverently on men's shoulders as a token of their sanctity (Wesley); the holiest things demanding the most reverent handling, as the later death when the ark was set on a new cart contrary to the command (2 Sam 6:6); reverence, not convenience, governing the carriage of the sacred.

schools.summary: The dedication of the altar — 'the princes offered for dedicating of the altar in the day that it was anointed' (the altar, anointed and sanctified with the holy oil, Lev 8:10, now dedicated by the princes' gifts and the first solemn offerings upon it); not the first consecration (done by Moses and Aaron with the anointing oil) but a festal dedication by use, a 'handselling' of the altar into joyful service with thanksgiving (Wesley, Henry); each prince offering before the altar on behalf of and at the charge of his whole tribe, the gift the tribe's, presented by its head (Clarke); the kinds of gift — a silver charger and bowl and a golden spoon from each tribe, besides the sacrifices (Calvin); the altar, the centre of Israel's worship, honoured at its first use with the leaders' liberality.

dedication by joyful use

schools.summary: The order, one prince each day — 'they shall offer their offering, each prince on his day, for the dedicating of the altar'; Moses not knowing in what order they should offer, by birth or otherwise, till God directed, the matter settled by express divine word (Gill); the princes offering in the order of their camp and march — Judah first, then the rest as ranged about the tabernacle — not by birth, so Nahshon of Judah whose standard led offered first (Wesley, Keil); the day-by-day arrangement giving each tribe its own day of honour, prolonging the joy through twelve days, each offering received with full solemnity; God preventing all rivalry and confusion, none offering before his day, none slighted, every tribe an equal place; God ordering even the times of his people's giving.

for the dedicating of the altar

schools.summary: Judah offering first — 'he that offered his offering the first day was Nahshon the son of Amminadab, of the tribe of Judah' (he whose standard was pitched first, on the east toward the rising sun, offered first, the order of the camp determining the order of the dedication, Gill); the deeper ground — the oracle of dying Jacob, 'The scepter shall not depart from Judah' (Gen 49:10), though the time of its open fulfilment in the kingdom and in Christ was not yet come, Judah already holding the first place, a foreshadowing of the royal dignity (Calvin); Nahshon the son of Amminadab in the genealogy of David and the Messiah (Mt 1:4), the offering led by an ancestor of Christ; the tribe from which the King would come going first in worship as in the host.

schools.summary: The silver vessels and the meat offering — 'one silver charger... one silver bowl of seventy shekels... both full of fine flour mingled with oil for a meat offering'; the charger a deep dish such as used for kneading the paste of the meal offering, the bowl a basin to receive the blood of the sacrifices (Ex 27:3, Clarke); both of silver, belonging to the service of the altar and court not the inner sanctuary whose vessels were of gold (Gill, Wesley); the weights after the shekel of the sanctuary, the charger 130 shekels, the bowl 70, costly vessels of solid silver; filled with the meal offering of fine flour and oil, the offering of the fruits of the ground, thankful dedication of God's gifts; the typology — the fine flour without leaven pointing to Christ the bread of life, mingled with the oil of the Spirit.

one silver bowl

schools.summary: The golden spoon of incense — 'one spoon of ten shekels of gold, full of incense'; the Hebrew caph a small censer or pan on which the incense was put and burned (Ex 25:29, Clarke), its weight ten shekels, about four ounces of gold (Gill); this vessel, unlike the silver charger and bowl, of gold, for it pertained to the incense burned before the LORD, the things nearest the holy service of the more precious metal; 'full of incense' — the spoon filled with the holy incense, the sweet spices burned to God; the meaning — incense the figure of prayer, the prayers of the saints ascending as a sweet savour (Ps 141:2, Rev 8:3), and of the intercession of Christ making the people's prayers acceptable; the tribes bringing their offerings in the order in which they encamped about the tabernacle (Clarke).

themes: one spoon of ten shekels of gold

schools.summary: The burnt offering — 'one young bullock, one ram, one lamb of the first year, for a burnt offering' (the bullock of three years old, as the Targums say, brought with a ram and a lamb, Gill); the nature — wholly consumed upon the altar, ascending entire to God, the offerer keeping no part, expressing total consecration and self-dedication, the giving up of all to the LORD; the fitness at the dedication — the first great act of worship on the newly hallowed altar the entire surrender of the best of herd and flock, a token that the people and all they had were wholly his; the typology — the unblemished victims wholly offered pointing to Christ, who offered himself without spot to God (Heb 9:14); each prince bringing the same burnt offering, every tribe alike consecrated.

themes: one young bullock, one ram, one lamb
for a burnt offering

schools.summary: The sin offering joined to the joyful dedication — 'one kid of the goats for a sin offering' (a young goat brought as a sin offering alongside the burnt offering and peace offerings); though these offerings were by way of thanksgiving, to express joy at the erection of the tabernacle and altar, yet a sin offering was joined, since even in their best and most joyful services there might be some sin and imperfection needing atonement (Gill); no act of worship, however glad or costly, so pure it does not need the cleansing of an atoning sacrifice, the holiest dedication accompanied by a sin offering lest the offering be defiled by the offerer's sin; man cannot bring even a thank offering without the taint of sin, ever coming by way of atonement; the typology — the sin offering pointing to Christ, made sin for us (2 Cor 5:21).

schools.summary: The peace offerings completing Judah's gift — 'two oxen, five rams, five he goats, five lambs of the first year: this was the offering of Nahshon' (the peace offerings far outnumbering the single victims of the burnt and sin offerings); more numerous because of the peace offering the offerers partook, the princes, priests, and people making a feast before the Lord out of them, eating in fellowship and joy, so many beasts needed for so great a company (Wesley); here gathered all the sorts of offering — the meat (and drink) offering, the burnt offering of consecration, the sin offering of atonement, and the peace offering of communion, the dedication embracing the whole range of worship (Gill); the fitness of ending in the peace offering, communion after consecration and atonement, the shared feast of reconciliation; the typology — Christ the peace offering, our peace (Eph 2:14).

schools.summary: Issachar offering on the second day — 'on the second day Nethaneel the son of Zuar, prince of Issachar, did offer' (Issachar offering next after Judah, as in the camp Issachar pitched next to Judah on the east under the same leading standard, the order of the dedication following the order of the encampment, Gill); each prince having his own appointed day, Issachar's prince coming forward on the second, neither before his time nor after; the equality in the repetition — Issachar bringing the very same offering as Judah, in nothing less or more, the tribes differing in number and dignity yet standing alike at the altar, none exalted above another in worship; Scripture setting down Issachar's offering in full though the same as Judah's, honouring each tribe by its own record.

schools.summary: Issachar's silver vessels and meat offering — 'one silver charger... one silver bowl... both full of fine flour mingled with oil for a meat offering' (the same as the first prince's gift, Num 7:13, Gill); the silver charger of 130 shekels, the silver bowl of 70, both filled with fine flour and oil for a meat offering, in every particular the same as Nahshon's; the meat offering — the fruits of the ground offered to God, owning him as the giver of the people's bread and dedicating their sustenance, the oil betokening the grace that sanctifies; God requiring of each tribe the same offering, neither more from the great nor less from the small, the equality teaching the equality of the tribes before God in worship; Scripture recording Issachar's vessels in full, honouring the tribe's devotion by its own naming.
